summary: O'Neil critiques a Washington Post food columnist who writes about how diets statistically don't work, only to undermine her own argument by describing her personal weight loss success. The columnist acknowledges that diets are shams that exploit people's desires, yet concludes by saying she personally diets because 'being fat made me unhappy.' O'Neil argues this creates a contradictory message that implies weight is a personal choice and that dieting does work if you just try hard enough. She calls out the cognitive dissonance of simultaneously debunking diets while engaging in fat shaming.
key_points: The columnist correctly identifies that diets statistically don't work and exploit people's insecurities
Despite this evidence-based critique, the columnist undermines her argument by sharing her own dieting 'success'
The piece implies fatness is a choice and a source of unhappiness that individuals should fix
O'Neil identifies the contradiction: you can't admit diets don't work while simultaneously fat shaming
money_quotes: a food columnist writes about how diets are shams, how they statistically don't work, and they play on people's desires to live a different life – all good things to point out – and yet – yet!!
So, we are left to assume that it is after all a choice, and that dieting *does* work, but just not commercial dieting? Give me a break lady.
When you admit that diets don't work, stop with the fat shaming.
key_insight: It's hypocritical to cite evidence that diets don't work while simultaneously reinforcing the narrative that weight is a personal failing to be corrected.
